case_title: NATASHA WILLIAMS VS. CITY NATIONAL BANK, A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL

docket_entries.description: NOTICE TO PLAINTIFF
ORDER GRANTING DEFENDANT CITY NATIONAL BANK'S PETITION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION AND MOTION TO DISMISS OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, STAY THE ACTION

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT CITY NATIONAL BANK'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS GRANTED AND THIS CASE IS STAYED PENDING THE COMPLETION OF THE ARBITRATION PROCEEDINGS. THE BANK SATISFIED ITS BURDEN OF SHOWING THAT MS. WILLIAMS AGREED TO THE TERMS OF THE ARBITRATION AGREEMENT IN HER EMPLOYMENT APPLICATION. ORDER SIGNED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES AND/OR ORDER FOR COMPLETE RULING). JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jan-26-2018 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-22-2017 Continued To Feb-14-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-22-2016 Continued To Oct-11-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION / Notice Of And Petition To Compel Arbitration And Motion To Dismiss Or, In The Alternative, Stay Action; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Supporting Declarations Of Kathryn Bridenbaugh And William Davis Harn
Off Calendar Nov-10-2016 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. Defendant City National Bank's motion to compel arbitration is granted and this case is stayed pending the completion of the arbitration proceedings. The Bank satisfied its burden of showing that Ms. Williams agreed to the terms of the arbitration agreement in her employment application. California case law enforces arbitration agreements in employment applications even when an application is not signed by the employer since the employer's intent to be bound by the agreement is evidenced by its use of the application. (Cruise v. Kroger Co. (2015) 233 Cal. App. 4th 390). Assuming without deciding that the arbitration agreement has a high degree of procedural unconscionability, plaintiff Natasha Williams has not shown any substantive unconscionability and thus the arbitration agreement is not unconscionable. The only grounds asserted by Ms. Williams why the arbitration agreement is substantively unconscionable is the attorney's fees provision and the absence of any provision regarding judicial review. Unlike the attorney's fees provision in Triveldi v. Curexo Technology Corp. (2010) Cal.App.4th 387, 394-396, the attorney's fees provision in the arbitration agreement agreed to by Ms. Williams does not expose Ms. Williams to a fee award that is contrary to FEHA or other applicable law. The absence of a judicial review provision in the arbitration agreement agreed to by Ms. Williams necessarily means that the judicial review provisions of the California Arbitration Act apply. No court has held that the applicability of such provisions renders an arbitration agreement substantively unconscionable. Order signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
